column1: Thread Content
Any issues sowing winter wheat after winter oats? Not as good as a broadleaf break crop I know. There hasn’t been Wheat here for a long time.
I will be going with a fair share of Dawsum after beans.  Seemed to do well in general and performed extremely well in some dodgy fields that were surrounded by trees .
yeeeeee winter wheat 2024...

we are normaly late sowing our winter wheat, going to mix it up this year and sow early. what would be earliest reasonable date for starting??? Would you plant winter wheat before planting winter barley?
Yes 7th of October
With the current weather forecast I’m sorry I didn’t sow a bit already. Only T-0 if there’s rust. A lot of our higher yielding wheats in last few years were drilled 13th-20th sept.
Lad we get seed off rang last week to ask us if we wanted seed as reports are it's scarce to be got already. Just a heads up for lads
Sowed my wheat today. I like the look of that Dawsum, lovely looking grain. Conditions nice once sun came out. Every tractor in the parish was going long hours this weekend.
Plough today, dry tomorrow, sow Tuesday.
What kind of herbicide programme do ye use? Pre or post emerge? Its my first time on the winter wheat.
Strip tilling for the first time here, Dawsum going in
A neighbor getting bean ground ready for Graham winter wheat . The cultivator is on hire .
An early start, will we get 26ha sown by midnight?

Winter wheat (Dawsum) & winter barley (Tardis) going in around the Goldcrop plots today. Early start and 900ish plots sown today by the trusty 3600. The number one contractor in E Cork sowing around the plots this evening.
Got a bit of wheat sowed yesterday, following OSR, I wasn't brave enough to grub and decided to plough
Two drains blocked since after the harvest, oh the fun.
Getting some good of the newer one, but still not fully clear. View attachment 20231012_142950.mp4
Spraying the winter wheat with Stomp and DFF.
What kind of seeding rate should dawsum  going  in now be at?
Ploughing for wheat after beans, it is far from ideal.
Dawsum going in at 170 Kg/ Ha.
late nights and early mornings getting through it, torp going in for a customer 360 seeds m2 tonight
ground is better today than last week, I think
Hoping to get 9ha done by noon.

Have the last 32 acres of wheat to go in this morning and of course the main ram broke on the drill last night !! Hopefully be going by 9:30 , it’s a quick field to work and I have about 5 acres ready to sow . Will need until 1 to get it in but think rain to us to hit a little earlier
Into the 3rd field, but will have to go back and plough the headlands when sown.

Just finished ploughing a heavier field destined for wheat, was committed to turning it over because it’s had chicken litter. It needs a dry day before sowing, never mind a soaking just after the drill leaves. Weather forecasting is a pain, at the end of last week, all this week here was to be dry…then it changed to rain on Thursday- so we spread the dung at the weekend- plough Monday, dry out Tuesday and sow Wednesday was the plan 🤬

Got 33 acres of champion in , didn’t get going sowing until 11 and just got out the gate at 2:20 before it started spilling rain, if we had another run around the field I don’t think we would have pulled the drill around , headland we’re tacky enough after the hedgecutter man but came up ok and upped the seed rate to 220kg to compensate for any losses.
Few fields local, will be worse tomorrow.
14hours difference in planting between the green in the distance and the just emerged in the foreground. Photo doesn’t really do it justice

Believe it or not there is a nice bit of sowing happening around here this evening. Two places would be dry out and regardless of how the weather this week pans out it will be grand, one other place a good bit heavier
fortnight yesterday since it was sowed, glad to see it appear
Got a start made today Not all dry tho

Evening In people’s opinions and experience how much hardship will wheat take sowed in to nice conditions x rape but springs and high volumes of unwanted rain ya would hardly walk it sowed 2 weeks soon I know if barley was sowed there it be a write off !!
If it dried up I would stick in a second winter wheat. There is going to be a shortage of spring barley seed and I have have enough area destined for Beans .
The last of the wheat is up nicely, but it might be a few days before we can sow the rest.
The only non-inversion tillage I did this year, it looks well considering.
Spread a few slug pellets on the non-inversion tilled wheat this evening.
In what way do you think the slats are not as nice gone? Coming from 8's to the older one piece slat, I am not completely convinced either. More broken finish but possibly not as inverted as level.

Planted a small wheat trial this morning, land was just fit. Only for the patch being on a rise and being after beans I wouldn’t have been able to go. Land is just so wet around with a lot of springs flowing. No headlands involved either so no trampling. It’s a bit that won’t need to be done the next day.
Made a burst yesterday afternoon and today. Dawsum in after ley.
Graham going in today. Happy enough with the condition, all things considered.
Some losses on the winter wheat but not too bad, still half hopeful of getting some more sown if next week comes good, but it is also looking like more spring barley.

I laid out a few slates with meusli under them in our wheat to test for slug presence 2 days ago. The crop was sown into disced bean stubble that was only sprayed off 4-5 days previous so I was worried they'd be an issue with all the trash. Out of 5 slates only one had 3 slugs, the others varied between 0-2. I was expecting to see a lot more so I'm happy with that. The crop isn't looking as pretty as with @gone however
A member of our group put out traps to see what sort of population there was.

All quiet on the western front. Two drier fields were finished yesterday with Graham. All the wettter fields were sown in a tight window before rain and the drier fields were left because even if it was left to spring we could be in early.  It is what it is now. The lorry driver that delivered the seed spread dung here as a boy soldier driving a Dual Power Massey 178 for the Hero Kelly .  He also drew in silage with a Zetor for Des Leybourne. The silage was made in a hole in the ground until one of the Neighbors snitched on us . He also drove the Zetor back to Tullow as an unlicensed 14 year old . Nearly as much illegal activity in the 1970’s as in what @Bencroy area has now .

@Bog Man ,we dont do anything illegal, 🤣its just  we dont draw attention  to ourselves and its grand and nobody notices yan photo you took at the locks that time  is higher today,our women won a Connacht title yesterday. your ground mustn't be to bad when the loader is in the field, will straw be scare next year with so little winter crops in and providing it all grows after the wet
Don’t want to be alarming fellas 🤣 but I’m thinking bout a bitta Dawsum
Dawsum going in after beans , wanted to min till but the weather didn’t allow , 188kg/ha

I have a field of beet that will be pulled the 1st week in December it's normally mid January when a field would be cleared and it goes for SB which is a bit of a waste of a break crop, how early in the new year could you chance WW and what would the cut off date be.....
Was it @Barrowsider that said last year he was never happy with winter wheat harvested the same year it’s sown, I could have tagged wrong person too

We've had sw the last 3 years for seed after beet. Works in well because we leave beet in the ground till the spring. 2021 was the best year, cleared 4t with a variety called Duncan. This year and last were just over the 3.5t mark with Escape in 2022 and Fixum in 2023. Golden spring wheat straw is an easy sell to any lad using a diet feeder.
Supper time for slugs

Making a start ploughing for Dawsum wheat. Most of it turning up well but some areas are tougher and damper due to clay content. Ploughing the tougher areas first and it’ll get a couple of days before planting. Pellets were applied yesterday and no shortage of dead slugs on the surface this morning.
Threw out a kg of pellets yesterday too on the back of your suggestion. Glad I did it when you see the results today. Happy enough to press it too.
Try old forager knifes work a treat last for ages
Dawsum sown 18 days just peeping, crows gone rioting on it the last 2 days.
View attachment IMG_2864.mov Dawsum wheat going in this evening following Caprice spring beans. Land has dried out really well over the two past days since it was turned up to the air.
Sown Wednesday. Forecast looks dry. Crows are rampant and so are slugs. Is the loader allowed if you have pallets forks on??

I had a shirt and tie on when ploughing during the week, @Bog Man style in case anyone called to inspect the work. It also protrays the image of a tillage man doing well and it upsets the cow man that might have an eye on the land. If you have no furniture, paint the front door.
Put on Tower and aphicide on the Graham today. Conditions great after the dry week. Most of it still there despite 290mm rain since it was sown.
Looking to take advantage of this rare dry spell to sow the last bit of Graham. What sort of seed rate are people using this time of year? Crows usually a problem round here

I am hoping to sow in the next couple of days and I will be going with a good bit lower a seed rate. I will be aiming to sow @ 215kg/ha or about 420/440 seeds/M² I don't have an exact 1,000 grain weight.

Getting ready for the last wheat plots of the year, 2000 of them to go here. 10mm of rain between Sunday night and last night was very unwelcome. Plenty of moisture in the soil but it should dry now it’s been turned up.
First time I have had a serious look at the first Dawsum wheat sown and if I take the right tablets and use the good glasses I might actually have a semi respectable crop in some fields .

A beautiful calm, crisp sunny day. Got the wheat Septoria plots in following WOSR. Approx 2000 plots, all in sequential order. 5-6 acres in all, I’ll put thread over the whole area to keep the crows away.
Finished the wheat yesterday. Black with crows this morning 😡
Dawsum sown 4 days, not hanging around. Ten crows won’t see Christmas.
Frost on the ploughing tis morn hard to break down the lumps , hope der isn’t too much rain for next wk on top of what’s being sat into very marginal conditions especially in headlands

I must crops if it wasn't for "people" like yourself "we'd never have new varieties of feck all to grow that's going the extra mile .. Out of interest when your growing a trial plot of new varieties how on earth do put on fertilizer and crop protection products , I take it each individual plot just gets the same as in you fert and spray the same as you would for one complete field or do you have to do individual plots????
Would ye be brave enough
@Big Vern this is me spraying the variety plots today, as you can see the tramlines run at 90 degrees to the plots so they can all get the exact same treatment.

Crops, Thanks for all that info, "you" just think that the agronomist comes each year and says yeah yeah we've got a new variety you could try next year it'll do this or that , it makes me realise how much work people like Pearce sees put in (through) their network of trial farmers to ensure that there are new varieties, that disease resistance and yeald improves Top work fella. Edit you clearly have better weather there than here ... It's minus 4 ATM..... All the floods down on the levels are like skating rinks....
December wheat madness
Graham going in at 215kg/Ha.
Finishing off the last of the winter wheat, I have some company ploughing.
2023 over and done, and may the likes of this bastard of year not be seen again.
I see a crow 🐦‍⬛ in the field. Don’t shoot him .
Winter wheat getting 2L of Tower pre-emerge.
Non-inversion tillage wheat getting 2L of Tower and 2kg of Combitop
Graham, sown 5 weeks. Happy to see rows of plants. Crows have taken some seeds and emerging plants but I'm happy enough with what is there, sown at 375 seeds/sq metre.
3 weeks and 3 days later the Champion has emerged. Crows making a scratch here and there but nothing of major concern.
The Tower is working, but probably still will need a tidy up in the spring.
Was that it on dd a while back @Oakley

Wheat plots sown 4 weeks emerging nicely. On a bit of crow duty this morning, commercial wheat is emerging well but it also makes it easier for them to find the plants. Executed a few which keeps them away for a couple of days.
Jeepers,I thought John McCririck was dead!!
Dawsum wheat sown on the 24th November making a welcome appearance this morning.
11th of November sown champion in first 2 pics and the 2nd two are 7th of November sown Dawsum, there is/was twice the vigour in the Dawsum

We had a wheat variety with similarly bad straw in trials, it yielded extremely well in official trials in 2022 but lodged in official trials in 2023. I decided to stop it progressing any further once I saw the first plot of it lodged, it hadn’t been harvested yet but there was no point fooling myself or anyone else. I’m testing varieties with 20 years, it’s a pet hate of mine when people market/sell varieties that haven’t been properly tested and have known serious weaknesses.
The wheat planted on the 2nd of December is up nicely and what was planted on the 4th is just peeping.
Winter wheat looking well from over the ditch yesterday

Before Christmas I was happy with our wheat sown 27th Nov. Had a crow banger set up and it was doing a great job keeping them away. Went out today only to discover somebody had turned it off over the holiday. Crows have had a field day ever since it seems. If it was causing a disturbance I would have appreciated a call to say so and we could have worked around it
Just when you mention crow bangers, which is the best make/model to buy. Thanks

This Champion is after taking a fair hammering from wind and rain the last 2 weeks. Hopefully we're past that now. I was a small bit concerned where the tractor drove when I rolled it, but there are plants emerging. Few more weeks you'd hardly spot them. The beauty of the 12m roller is you're on a tramline every 2nd run.
Late sown winter wheat is growing away nicely, it’s good to have a bit of dry weather to take the pressure off the crops.
Thinking to go with Tower on the end Nov sown Wheat. No frost forecast for next week but looks to get very cold again thereafter. Would it be risky ?

In general I would find Tower quite safe especially on wheat. If I hadn't everything already done I would be taking this opportunity. Just note that Tower has a few weeds it is weak on and it may need a follow up spray in the spring.
Mid  November sown Dawsum got generic defy and dff today. Ground was very good to travel.
The milder weather is very welcome for the late sown wheat. Leaf 3 starting to emerge.
Despite the late sowing date, I’m very happy with how the wheat disease plots have developed, all 2000 of them. Finally took down the anti-crow thread today. Sown 27th November.
Dawsum. I am the Jethroe Tull of mintill.
Done the little bit of wheat we have Not all sunshine

If I take the happy pill and rose tinted glasses there is a crop in. Most of it . Putting in the tramlines with the gps tractor so that I can start fertilizer . I should have loaded up with Ceraide for the thin spots .

Calm days are still scarce. The late November sown Dawsum wheat getting its first taste of fertiliser. No weeds sprayed yet, that’ll be the next job. It was a hard winter on weeds too so they aren’t plentiful. Snow on the Knockmealdowns in the distance.
Last May there was CAN sitting on the ground for weeks and when it eventually washed in it just made weak straw
What are the headlands like, min till of some sort is it?
Gave the winter wheat its feed of compound yesterday evening. Tramlines in good order but crop is thin enough looking. This field might be the worst one, but not by much.
Picking stones this evening . Fendt with stone fork on the front .

About 10 days since the end of November Dawsum received its first N. It has responded quickly. Next job is the weed spray, I think it’ll be a good SU + Pixxaro. Virtually no Annual Meadow Grass thankfully, probably due to controlling it well in previous years. The crop isn’t big and lush but there’s no bare areas thankfully.
Nearly finished picking stones on the Winter Wheat. The second picture is a field that we will probably sow barley in .
Mine has perked up a bit over the last week or 10 days since it got compound. I felt it was very thin all winter but filling in well now.
Out on the wheat today. Trying to give it a kick on with some 10s. Very hungry looking but all in all it wasn't a bad take considering the conditions in the winter
First time at the winter wheat, it got its first split of n in compound just wondering what growth stage do lads stick on the main split? As in is it too early to be doing it now?
Easy see difference in soil type and the drains in the second picture.
No WW here this year but was told you should go with 3x10 method. 3 splits, 10 March, 10 April and 10 May in general.
I was driving through Wicklow today and was admiring lovely green crops of Wheat and Barley and then I realized I had my sunglasses on .
What do most people do for PGR on WW? Is CCC and Modus enough or would some people go with CCC twice and modus with the second round?
1st year at Strip Tilling, Dawsum has its good and bad patches
Spraying wheat with shortner trace elements and duplosan for volunteer beans and it is after making a downpour which was not forecast until 5pm. Very depressing.
Make that 2 downpours! Worst part is I had half a tank left when it started raining and i have to put it out on to a saturated crop, what a waste of money

Finishing off spraying that field of wheat today and the beans are visibly crumpled over so at least that worked, will be interesting to see if the pgr worked despite multiple downpours during and after.

A second run of N on the late sown Dawsum, early this morning while it was still calm. The sun appeared briefly. I don’t think it’ll have any problem getting washed in…. A few thin patches from slugs but I’m happy overall. I do take off the loader on occasion.
During the week All the Winter Wheat is sprayed with 255g of Broadway star and 1L/ha CCC . No autumn herbicide and he said some of the fields had virtually no weeds.

Walking the wheat here today and there is a good bit of the tips of previous leaf scorched looking. It was only sprayed for first time last week so it couldn't be scorch from spray. Is it just weather related or could it be a deficiency/disease?
I have the winter wheat and barley costs sorted out . All I need is a Time Machine to go back to 1983 .
No sun tan lotion and all these chemicals. They could have sent me into Chernobyl and I would have had it cleaned up in a week .
Round two for fertilizer. Needs a spraying now next. The weeds have come on big time
Mine has woken up over the last fortnight. Sprayed today. PGR, Mancozin, fungicide and Avena for lots of oats after coming through from last years crop.
Had you the Thiopron liquid sulphur in it aswell?
Nope, didn’t have that.

Bringing the winter wheat up to 160 units . The Fendt has an error code so the TW15 stepped in . She is wired for the sprayer and spreader . We replaced a speed sensor a few years ago and it seems to be the same problem .
What are ye thinking of on T1. Local merchant telling me go with Ascra and save Revystar for T2. Any opinions either way?
Revystar T1 and inatreq T2 is my plan.
1st spray for the wheat today.
Dawsum at gs 32 but third last leaf not quite emerged, probably early next week

Sprayed mine today. Ascra .9 L/ha ,folpet 1.5 L / ha, axial .8. My 15 acre sprayer did 17 acres, sprayer wasn't working right as nozzles were blocking up with residue from the last spray. My spray programme for this field isn't going according to plan! Volunteer beans I thought were dieing are standing back up again So I guess the pgr that was in with the herbicide won't work either given the flood of rain I got after spraying.
In North Kerry. Just two growers with winter wheat near us almost everything else is spring barley. Yeah that field is poisoned in canary grass.
Used happen sometimes with my old Berthoud. Not an issue now with the Amazone though. Think the boom circulation system allows the boom to wash better as no dead ends

I have a Berthoud with boom circulation and I still get a few blocked nozzles when using glyphosate. I find it’s nearly always the three or four nozzles at at both ends of the boom so I usually put nozzles filters on them and it’s a good help. As said before Mancozin seems to be the main culprit.

Autumn applied IPU used be bad for staying in the lines . If stopping due to rain we can run clean water through the lines . We would often take off the stopends and flush out the lines before starting roundup .

I have a lot of random plants scattered through the field much more advanced than the main crop. Thought there was an infestation of wild oats early on but appears to be maybe a different variety of seed? Dawsum was the variety. Does Dawsum have awns?
Thanks for the explanation. Yeah looks a bit messy alright but at least it has recovered in colour in the last week.

Mine escaped it this year, bar a bit that is really sheltered beside some trees where it is showing up in some patches. What sort of a yield effect could you expect with wheat? Similar to Winter Barley? There 2 years ago I was hit with a ton/acre on one field of 18 acres. My winter barley averaged approx 4 ton/acre and the badly affected field did 3 ton/acre. None of it got an aphacide but the 4 ton stuff was sown around Oct 10 and the 3 ton field the last days of September. Sowed my wheat this year first week of October. Fingers 🤞
T1
Going to be some crack with these trams come harvest.
Champion. It is tall and will need terpal
Dawsum. Good and steady but more septoria.

I must be at the right thing this evening if you are all at it. T1 and PGR going onto Mid October sown trials. No radio or phone being used while at this job, 4 separate trials with some sections to be left untreated, easy to make a mess of it. About 100 different varieties so matching fungicide timing to leaf emergence is a fair bit of a compromise. It looks like the fungicide will get a fair test tomorrow….
Dawson and graham yesterday

The flag leaf is emerging on mine. Looks like I went too early with my T1, must have been leaf 4 not 3 I sprayed. Anyway, its 3.5 weeks since it was sprayed should I hold tough till flag leaf is fully out or drive on the next dry day? By the way the PGR and the weed spray I applied and got a flood of rain after worked as you can clearly see height difference and beans growing at corners where I shut off the inside boom.
At this stage I would hold but maybe I may be wrong
Walked it again this evening and some plants have flag leaf fully emerged now. Going to spray either tomorrow or Friday now I'd say. Going with 3/4 rate Revystar and Folpet, with some B Evergreen.

Upper leaves look good but lower leaves are rotten. There is a lot of 6 row barley coming up through it, assume it's volunteers and it didn't come in the seed? eventhough the previous crop was beans? Looks very unsightly. And you can see the volunteer beans that were missed at the ins and outs

Winter wheat untreated here looks the same as treated at present, loads of Septoria below Leaf 3 but top 3 leaves still clean. We know that’s not going to last so there’s a lot of Septoria to pop up yet….
The year is moving on. This is Graham,  is it earlier than normal?
Sprayed Dawsum T2 with aquino turret . And arizona . He got stopped with rain earlier but finishing it now .

Took a look at a neighbors wheat trials. The control plots got a weed spray and 180kg/ha of urea and nothing else. All the usual varieties won’t be worth cutting..but there was a couple of outstanding new varieties. First is.. It’s resistant to bydv, mosaic, orange blossom midge and eyespot.
Second variety is resistant to bydv, mosaic and midge. Both varieties are supposed to be available for this autumn all going well.
The winter wheat variety plots have bulked up nicely
Honestly never seen such a winter crop of barley as one of my neighbours has this year, must take a pic

It's coming up 3 weeks since my T2 on the wheat. Seems to be a bit of disease showing on the flag leaf. Dawsum is the variety. See some talk online to bring forward the T3 head spray to help protect the flag leaf in feed wheat. Heads are almost fully emerged but not flowering yet. Any thoughts on this?

Cant seem to find it now about bringing timing forward a bit. I'm sure I didn't imagine it! I do see a couple of mentions of going again with an sdhi as well as the azole if pressure is high. Vimoy and Univoq were mentioned. Adding significantly to the cost no doubt.

Sorry not a wheat grower, but I was watching Olly blogs on youtube. He was at a UPL open day. They where trialling Iodus fungicide put on at T0 and it had kept crop clean through to booting, it is supposed to work a bit like a vaccine ,tricks the plant into thinking it has the disease. Thought it was quite interesting is there any products like that for sale here? Variety was Dawsum
I was talking to one of the biggest independent trial companies in Ireland and they were testing biologicals for companies and none of them work consistently.

Between septoria, bybv, yellow tips on leaves, volunteer beans, volunteer hybrid barley a few wild oats and Canary grass in corners, I must say this field of winter wheat has really tested me. Last spray today thanks be to God. Anthers out on 50 to 75 percent of plants.
This picture doesn't do justice to how crap it looks driving through it.
This is a better picture of the yellow tipping.

The yellow tipping or what you think is septoria might be ascochyta. Are the marks the same as this? Symptoms of ascochyta leaf scorch (spot) in cereals | AHDB Unlike many other foliar diseases of cereals, ascochyta leaf scorch (spot) is sporadic and rarely severe. ahdb.org.uk I’ve come across a few bits of it the last few days, in Dawsum mainly so far.

Some patches are definitely bydv as there is stunted plants. But in the main yeah it looks a bit like those pictures alright and it is Dawsum I have also, whether variety has any bearing on that. I doubt it's any nutrient deficiency anyway as have been generous with both macro and micro nutrients.
A field of Dawsum. Very little disease and three weeks after it was sowed it was a near right off. It has 165 units of Sul Can.
Willow Herb is getting to be a problem. Not sure is it because I am tilling shallower than I used to. He wiped it in Spring Wheat last year but the same spray this year had no effect .
Wheat got its head spray This eve, a mix of maxentis and epsocombitop. Plenty septoria in it despite full rate revystar and inatreq.
Wheat not looking good at all. First time for me growing it. I followed agronomist recommendations to the letter, was it just down to bad timing I wonder it’s hit so bad?

Ours is something similar to your pictures there. I see on the UK farmers forum they say that the Dawsum over there had a bad colour and a lot of yellow tipping which was the biggest problem with ours. Though I think I may have been a bit light on my T2 as pointed out here and my timings were all a bit early too I reckon.

I just don’t know enough about it myself to make any changes to what’s recommended to me. I find the sprays so hard to follow due to changing rates, ingredients and trade names every year. I suppose there is no easy way to learn it only by my mistakes. That said I could fall into the same trap next year.
Head spray done on the late November sown Dawsum. Protioconazole + Combitop. Hopefully gate closed until harvest.

@Blackwater boy It’s arguably worse than the October sown stuff. Struggle to keep the top two leaves clean. More rainfall in this location and I was probably a few days late with T1. No T0. Had a wheat breeder across yesterday, another one today and another one on Tuesday. All here to look at Septoria screening plots. Finding cleaner genetics is a major aim. 3 different varieties pictured. All October sown, zero fungicide input.
Wow massive difference, what are the varieties?

I was just having a discussion the other day about wheat. The rotation would have meant us potentially putting more in this Autumn, I was actually pondering WB instead. The margins with using expensive chemicals at high rates not doing an acceptable job are debatable… Do you think the loss of Bravo has made any discernible difference?
Wheat has melted in these parts the last few weeks, lack of sun no help but mainly septoria has ruined them.
Cut graham yesterday 4.7 @ 19% Didn’t look very ripe. Stuck combine into another bit this morning and it was 27.
Took a sample there today, 28 moisture. Will have to give it a week at least. Lots of small grain in it so not so hopeful on the yield.
Dawsum. Around 20% ish
Snap

Mine is Dawsum sown mid October, it was 20-21 yesterday down to 18-19 today. Got Septoria pretty bad is going to finish up at about 3.5 ton/ac. It’s a pity as it looked to be better than that all year. Wheat is new to nee too, fella told me drive on at 21 especially with the mixed weather next week.

I must be doing the right thing if ye lads are at it. Dawsum sown 10th October. Just cutting around the trial plots. Didn’t test the moisture but I’d expect 21-22% based on the feel of it. Nice enough sample in this, the later sown wheat has more small grains in it.
Improving with every bin, and still on the headland, volunteer beans an all!

Yea I was surprised by the KPH. We haven’t grown wheat with a long time so I wouldn’t be a good judge of it. It looked a thick enough crop on the header anyway. Not too much wheat cut around here yet.
Finished our bit of wheat today, did 4.4 ton/acre. Not bad I suppose given the weather and disease pressure this year,  but it was first wheat after beans in one of my better yielding fields.
Started this afternoon, moisture around 20%.
That’s the Dawsum cut, have 2 fields of champion but they are a few days from being ripe.
Started at the plots this afternoon too.
First impressions of the wheat plots are yield isn’t terrible (but they are plots so no weak areas) but kph is generally 4-5 points below where it should be.
Tried a bit of Dawsum 24.6 .
There is a savage crop of straw off that Dawsum. Doing around 15 bales per acre.

Wheat is yielding poorly around here. Most yielding either side of 3.5 T/ac. Similar to the winter barley the worst crops were hit with BYVD. Septoria and premature senescence did the rest. <4 T/ac wheat is no good, particularly on rented land. Difficult growing seasons and poor yields annd lower prices are inevitable some years but it’s the cost of production that’s killing the job at the moment.

If there was more of a market for rye it would be a serious contender to replace some wheat. Early sown, no take all, no bydv, modest rates of N and cheap and cheerful fungicide packages that work and yields like wheat.
Cutting champion today. It's the first properly ripe wheat we've cut so far. The Graham was 17 to 20 moisture but very green.  Dawsum is for the next fine day It's not quite ripe yet.
I did a hand sample of our champion this afternoon and it was 22.3 so you could definitely and on another point for a combine sample. Maybe the weekend for it.
Think this may be carlow 2nd best tillage farmer in action seen on Monday evening

Cut 25 acres of champion on Tuesday evening, well i should say I cut 22 acres . 3 acres of nothing , 2 acres of very thin and dirty wheat and 20 acres of very nice wheat . Did 3.9 . Next field of wheat is still 4 or 5 days away .
Cut Graham yesterday, second wheat and average in this field was 4.35t/ac. The overall average was 4.26t @ 20% moisture. I'm pleased with the result, some wheat is performing well in the area.
Very clean varieties are rare, this one stood out all year and still stood out as the combine went towards it today.

Finished up our wheat last night. An absolute pleasure to go through with the combine. We started with Graham that did 4.1 after spring osr and finished with Champion that did 4.5 after winter osr. What is the consciences on second wheats,.. we have being growing first wheats last few years,, with disappointing results for yield.. bear in mind we used home saved seed some of the years and this year went for certified seed for first wheats,, but still poorish results for yeilds.. im thinking our problem isnt tak all.. we would have missed one spray timing this year. and it was a very dificult spring and winter late sowing.. so considering a second wheat this year hoping we can manage it better and not over thinking about take all....

With winter barleys pretty decent and well capable of leaving a similar margin to wheats, rye also an option and with modern spring barleys yielding well there is a lot better and safer options than 2nd wheats. I don’t know anyone who grows 2nd wheats anymore.
I'm giving it another go but I think if next year is poor result I'll be dropping it for a while

I was going to chance a second wheat this year also. Think I could have done a few bits better than I did last year, sowing date, timings and rates of fungicides and maybe I held back a bit of N. What yield penalty could you be hit with on a 2nd wheat vs 1st wheat?

Don't want to tempt fate but, I have been at continuous WB on a block of 30 acres for the past 5 years and never used Latitude and have hit either 4 or more ton/acre every year off that block. Have been hit with BYDV and waterlogging in other parts that hurt yield but never noticed take all as being an issue. Sowing date always early October. Mostly it's hybrid barley i am using. Would variety or soil type have a bearing?

Found this on Bayer website, so weather also has a bearing: Autumn sowings are most at risk, especially in cold, wet seasons and in marginal soils with poor drainage and fertility. In good soils other micro-organisms and better root growth help reduce the damage caused by this disease. Optimum conditions for disease are a mild winter, a moist cool spring and a dry early summer. Light, alkaline soils can also be favourable for this disease ....... I use pig slurry ahead of sowing and this block of land is free draining with a good slope off it so no water logging, so I guess that helped. Good to know about PH also, soil would also be slightly acidic so that probably also a help.

Just after realising that my Acres rules means I am meant to have winter crops sown by mid October 'Geese and Swan' rules! Even though I have never seen either bird near my farm! Must check it out I'd there is any exemption allowed for WW sowing.
Has anyone grown any Fitzroy yet? It looks decent from the trial results.

We had a field that got take all and as you approached the gate there was less take all because of historical compaction. A local farmer used to flat roll winter wheat in the spring . Also if you use Ammonium Sulphate as the nitrogen source. Unless there is some reason I would go for a break crop as modern Prima donna varieties  do not like running on bald tyres .

Worked out average winter wheat yield here, adjusted to 20%mc it came in at 3.9tn/ac. The lowest average we ever had I think. A roasting from Septoria was the main cause, it was almost unstoppable. The majority was later sown (end of November) and it was a bit thinner than I would like for a late sown crop. Most of the wheat was inland and got a rub of that very heavy rain that hurt spring barley crops. Normally that location would have slightly lower Septoria pressure than the coast but it was the other way around this year, I think that heavy rain really drove the Septoria mad - it was the worst I ever saw. Straw was chopped and it was mostly for seed so there will still be a reasonably good margin. We will stick with winter wheat as it has been very good to us and is good for the tougher ground. New varieties coming for next autumn and hopefully new chemistry for 2026.
Going sowing 25 acres of Dawsum on heavy ground here this backend. Half afraid reading about the scalding lads got this year 🙈

It’s following oats on heavy ground. Have had winter and spring barley on this ground and neither ever done great. Winter oats done 3.7 at 17% moisture so I’m thinking wheat for a change. Some of the ground will be grubbed instead of ploughed roughly 3 acres
